title: parseJiraErrorMessage crashes with ClassCastException on non-primitive error values
description: Line 473: `obj["errors"]?.jsonObject?.entries?.forEach { (k, v) -> messages += "$k: ${v.jsonPrimitive.content}" }` calls `.jsonPrimitive.content` unconditionally on every value in the `errors` map. Jira's `errors` object can contain non-primitive values (e.g. arrays of constraint violations on custom fields). When `v` is a `JsonArray` or `JsonObject`, calling `.jsonPrimitive` throws `kotlinx.serialization.json.JsonException` at runtime. The outer `catch (_: Exception)` swallows it, but this means the entire error block returns null, so the user sees a generic 'Bad request (400)' instead of the real field-validation message.
evidence: obj["errors"]?.jsonObject?.entries?.forEach { (k, v) -> messages += "$k: ${v.jsonPrimitive.content}" }
suggestedFix: Guard each value: replace `v.jsonPrimitive.content` with `(v as? kotlinx.serialization.json.JsonPrimitive)?.content ?: v.toString()`. This is safe for primitives, arrays, and objects without throwing.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at line 473. `v.jsonPrimitive.content` is called unconditionally on every value in the `errors` map. kotlinx.serialization's `JsonElement.jsonPrimitive` throws `IllegalArgumentException` when the element is a `JsonArray` or `JsonObject`. The outer `catch (_: Exception)` at line 475 swallows the exception and returns null, so `parseJiraErrorMessage` returns null and callers surface the generic HTTP status string (e.g. 'Bad request (400)') instead of the real field-validation detail. This affects Jira Server workflows that use custom fields with array-valued errors (e.g. constraint-violation arrays). Severity is Medium rather than High: the exception is caught and handled gracefully (no crash, no data loss); the only consequence is degraded error messaging. The suggested fix (`(v as? JsonPrimitive)?.content ?: v.toString()`) is safe and non-breaking.
recommendedAction: Replace `v.jsonPrimitive.content` with `(v as? kotlinx.serialization.json.JsonPrimitive)?.content ?: v.toString()` at line 473. No passing tests would break; this purely improves error-message fidelity.

title: Fully dead service `TicketKeyCache` — registered in plugin.xml but never instantiated
description: `TicketKeyCache` is a `@Service(Service.Level.PROJECT)` class registered at plugin.xml:237. No production code anywhere calls `TicketKeyCache.getInstance(...)` or any of its six public methods (`extractKeys`, `get`, `isValidated`, `getUnvalidated`, `validateAndCache`, `clear`). The DI registration keeps the class compiling but nothing ever resolves the service. The feature it intended to support (ticket-key hyperlinking in the detail panel description/comments) was never wired up. `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` is also exclusively called from the dead `validateAndCache` (line 76) and has no other production callers.
suggestedFix: Delete `TicketKeyCache.kt` and remove the `<projectService serviceImplementation="com.workflow.orchestrator.jira.service.TicketKeyCache"/>` entry from plugin.xml (line 237). Optionally prune `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` and `TicketKeyInfo` if ticket-key hyperlinking is confirmed abandoned; otherwise wire the cache into the description/comment renderers in `TicketDetailPanel`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `TicketKeyCache` is registered as a project service in plugin.xml line 237, but zero production callers exist. Grep across the full project finds no `TicketKeyCache.getInstance(...)` calls and no calls to any of its public methods (`extractKeys`, `get`, `isValidated`, `getUnvalidated`, `validateAndCache`, `clear`) outside the class itself. `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` is also only referenced from within `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache`. The service wastes a registration slot and the platform instantiates it unnecessarily on project open. Medium severity is appropriate because it represents real waste (service registered + instantiated on every project open) even though no functional harm occurs.
recommendedAction: Delete TicketKeyCache.kt, remove the plugin.xml line 237 registration, and optionally prune `JiraApiClient.validateTicketKeys` and `TicketKeyInfo`. If ticket-key hyperlinking is planned, the cache should be wired into TicketDetailPanel description/comment renderers before being reinstated.

title: WorklogSection bypasses JiraService contract via raw JiraApiClient
description: WorklogSection retrieves JiraApiClient directly through JiraServiceImpl.getApiClient() and calls api.getWorklogs() on it, bypassing the JiraService interface entirely. The interface already exposes getWorklogs(issueKey) returning ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>, so this leaks the internal HTTP client into the UI layer and skips ToolResult error handling conventions.
evidence:
val jiraServiceImpl = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)
val apiClient = jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient()
...
val result = apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)
suggestedFix: Replace the two-step lookup with project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey). The return type changes from ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse> to ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>; adjust renderWorklogs() to consume WorklogData instead of the DTO. This removes the only remaining ui/ → api/ direct dependency.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real and is the architectural framing of the same issue as JIRA-CLE-9. WorklogSection.kt lines 61-71 bypass the `JiraService` interface and call raw `JiraApiClient` directly from the UI layer, violating the layering rule (ui/ must not reach api/ directly). `getApiClient()` is not part of the `JiraService` interface — it is an implementation-detail method on `JiraServiceImpl`. The finding is correctly flagged, though its claimed severity of High is slightly overstated: the bypass causes no data loss, no security issue, and the duplicate field mapping is purely a maintenance concern. Severity reassessed to Medium (architecture + maintainability, not user-visible wrong behavior).
recommendedAction: Route through `project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey)` and adapt `renderWorklogs()` to `WorklogData`. This removes the only remaining ui/ → api/ direct dependency in the jira module.

title: SprintDashboardPanel.onFilterClicked bypasses JiraService via raw JiraApiClient
description: onFilterClicked() retrieves the raw JiraApiClient from JiraServiceImpl.getApiClient() and calls api.searchByJql(jql, 50) directly. JiraService.searchTickets(jql, maxResults) is the correct service-layer entry point and already performs JQL validation (blank, length, control chars). Using the raw client skips those guards and the ToolResult contract.
suggestedFix: Replace api.searchByJql with service.searchTickets(jql, 50). Convert the returned ToolResult<List<JiraTicketData>> back to List<JiraIssue> only if needed by the cell renderer, or preferably refactor the list model to accept JiraTicketData (the shared core model). Remove the getApiClient() null-guard and the getApiClient() call entirely.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. SprintDashboardPanel.kt lines 711-718 call `service.getApiClient()` then `api.searchByJql(jql, 50)` directly. `JiraService.searchTickets(jql, maxResults)` at JiraServiceImpl line 1177-1220 has explicit JQL guards (blank check, MAX_JQL_LENGTH=2000, control-character check). The comment at line 709-710 acknowledges the bypass is intentional because the list model uses `List<JiraIssue>` (DTO) not `List<JiraTicketData>` (core model), and the cell renderer `TicketListCellRenderer` expects `JiraIssue`. The JQL risk is reduced since user-clicked saved filter JQL comes from Jira's own API, not agent input. Severity downgraded from High to Medium: the guards matter most for agent-originated JQL, and a proper fix requires either mapping `JiraTicketData` → `JiraIssue` or refactoring the list model.
recommendedAction: Either refactor the list model to accept `JiraTicketData` directly, or add an inline JQL validation before calling `api.searchByJql`. Using `service.searchTickets()` directly would require a `JiraTicketData` → `JiraIssue` conversion shim.

title: TicketTransitionServiceImpl secondary constructor has a circular dependency on JiraServiceImpl concrete class
description: The platform-invoked secondary constructor calls JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient() to obtain the JiraApiClient. This couples a core-interface implementation (TicketTransitionService) to a sibling concrete class (JiraServiceImpl), forming a within-module dependency chain that makes both services harder to test in isolation and violates the convention that services resolve through interfaces.
evidence:
constructor(project: Project, cs: CoroutineScope) : this(
    api = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient()
        ?: JiraApiClient(baseUrl = "", tokenProvider = { null }),
suggestedFix: Resolve the JiraApiClient by injecting it from plugin.xml using the <serviceImplementation> constructor parameter or by having the platform inject a JiraApiClient-provider service. Alternatively, have TicketTransitionServiceImpl construct its own JiraApiClient from CredentialStore and PluginSettings directly (same pattern as JiraSearchServiceImpl.createClient()), removing the JiraServiceImpl dependency.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. TicketTransitionServiceImpl.kt lines 66-74 show the secondary constructor directly calls `JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient()`. This creates a concrete-to-concrete dependency between two sibling service implementations in the same `:jira` module. The class KDoc at line 37-44 acknowledges this design choice ('no duplicate HTTP clients are created'). The fallback sentinel at lines 68-71 is a defensive measure. No circular dependency in the JVM sense (neither extends the other; both are constructed by the platform DI sequentially). The claimed fix (injecting from plugin.xml or constructing JiraApiClient from CredentialStore like JiraSearchServiceImpl) is technically correct and would make TicketTransitionServiceImpl independently testable, but current tests likely already provide the primary constructor directly. Medium severity confirmed.
recommendedAction: For testability, prefer constructing JiraApiClient directly from CredentialStore/PluginSettings in the secondary constructor (mirroring JiraSearchServiceImpl.createClient()), removing the JiraServiceImpl dependency.

title: validateTicketKeys throws IllegalArgumentException instead of returning ApiResult.Error, propagating uncaught through TicketKeyCache
description: Lines 529-532: when a key fails the anchored Jira key pattern, `validateTicketKeys` throws `IllegalArgumentException("Invalid Jira key: $k")` rather than returning `ApiResult.Error`. The only caller in production code is `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache` (line 76), which is a `suspend fun` that does NOT wrap the call in try/catch. The exception propagates as an unhandled coroutine exception, cancelling the enclosing coroutine scope and potentially crashing the feature silently. The function signature promises `ApiResult<...>` so callers do not expect an exception.
suggestedFix: Replace `throw IllegalArgumentException(...)` with `return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.VALIDATION_ERROR, "Invalid Jira key: $k")`. This keeps the function contract and lets callers handle it uniformly.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 529-532. `validateTicketKeys` throws `IllegalArgumentException` rather than returning `ApiResult.Error`. The sole production caller, `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache` at line 76, calls `client.validateTicketKeys(uncached.toList())` with no try/catch, meaning the exception would propagate as an unhandled coroutine exception. However, the practical impact is very limited: `TicketKeyCache.extractKeys()` at line 36 pre-filters all keys using `DEFAULT_REGEX = Regex("\\b([A-Z][A-Z0-9]+-\\d+)\\b")`, which matches the same key format as the `jiraKeyPattern` in `validateTicketKeys`. Keys that reach `validateTicketKeys` have already passed a compatible regex match, so the `throw` path is essentially unreachable in the normal flow. The contract violation (function signature implies no exception but can throw) is real and the fix is safe.
recommendedAction: Replace `throw IllegalArgumentException("Invalid Jira key: $k")` with `return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.VALIDATION_ERROR, "Invalid Jira key: $k")` at line 531. Also add a try/catch guard in `TicketKeyCache.validateAndCache` as defense-in-depth. No passing tests would break.

title: BranchingService.startWork / useExistingBranch crashes with NoSuchElementException on repositories with no configured remotes
description: Lines 118 and 214: `git.fetch(repo, repo.remotes.first(), emptyList())` calls `.first()` on `repo.remotes` with no guard. A freshly-cloned repository with no remotes configured, or any offline/disconnected clone where the remote list is transiently empty, throws `NoSuchElementException`. Because both call sites are inside a `try { ... } catch (e: Exception)` block the exception IS caught, but the error message 'Failed to checkout branch: null' (since `e.message` for `NoSuchElementException` is null) is exposed to the user with no actionable guidance.
evidence: val fetchResult = git.fetch(repo, repo.remotes.first(), emptyList())
suggestedFix: Add an explicit guard: `val remote = repo.remotes.firstOrNull() ?: return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NOT_FOUND, "No Git remote configured for repository '${repo.root.path}'.")` before the fetch call at both sites.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 118 and 214. `repo.remotes.first()` throws `NoSuchElementException` when `repo.remotes` is empty (e.g. a local-only repo or a transient VCS state during project indexing). Both call sites ARE inside `try { ... } catch (e: Exception)` blocks (lines 100-150 and 198-233), so there is no crash. The issue is solely UX: `NoSuchElementException.message` is null, so `e.message` is null, and the user sees 'Failed to checkout branch: null' with no actionable guidance. Severity is Low rather than High because the exception is always caught and the user is never left with a silent or partial failure — they get an error result. The fix (explicit `firstOrNull()` guard with a descriptive error message) is safe and non-breaking.
recommendedAction: At both line 118 and 214, replace `repo.remotes.first()` with an explicit `repo.remotes.firstOrNull() ?: return ApiResult.Error(ErrorType.NOT_FOUND, "No Git remote configured for repository '${repo.root.path}'.")` guard placed before the fetch call, outside the try/catch.

title: JiraServiceImpl.client property has an unsynchronized TOCTOU race on URL change under concurrent coroutines
description: Lines 96-112: `cachedClient` and `cachedBaseUrl` are both `@Volatile` but the check-then-act sequence at lines 104-110 (`if (url != cachedBaseUrl || cachedClient == null) { cachedBaseUrl = url; cachedClient = JiraApiClient(...) }`) is not atomic. Two coroutines on `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl`, both assign `cachedBaseUrl = url`, and both construct a new `JiraApiClient`. The second write wins for `cachedClient`, but the first coroutine proceeds using the `return cachedClient` value which is now the second instance. While this normally results in two identical clients rather than a crash, it means connection-pool resources are leaked and any in-flight request on the discarded client gets a new connection every time settings change rapidly.
suggestedFix: Use `@Synchronized` on the `client` getter or use a `@GuardedBy("this")` pattern: `@get:Synchronized private val client: JiraApiClient? get() = ...`. Alternatively, store a `Pair<String, JiraApiClient>` and use `@Volatile` on a single atomic reference.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 96-112. The check-then-act pattern on two `@Volatile` fields is not atomic: two coroutines on `Dispatchers.IO` can both observe `url != cachedBaseUrl` and both construct a new `JiraApiClient`. The second write to `cachedClient` wins, leaving the first coroutine using a client instance that is immediately abandoned (leaking its OkHttp connection pool). In practice, `JiraApiClient` instantiation is cheap (no network call) and this race only fires when settings change between concurrent requests — an extremely rare condition during normal use. There is no data corruption, no credential leak, and no crash. Severity is Low (not Medium) given the benign consequence. A `@Synchronized` getter or a `@Volatile` `Pair<String, JiraApiClient>` atomic reference would eliminate it.
recommendedAction: Annotate the `client` property getter with `@Synchronized`, or store a single `@Volatile` `Pair<String, JiraApiClient>?` reference and use `@Synchronized` on the update path. Either approach eliminates the connection-pool leak without changing observable behavior.

title: TicketDetailPanel.currentIssueKey written on EDT but read from IO thread without @Volatile — stale visibility
description: Line 105: `private var currentIssueKey: String? = null` is declared as a plain `var` (no `@Volatile`). It is written at line 126 on the EDT (`showIssue` is called from Swing event handlers) and read at lines 747 and 770 from `lazyScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO)` and `invokeLater` closures respectively. Without `@Volatile` (or confinement to a single thread), the JVM memory model does not guarantee that writes on the EDT are visible to threads on the IO thread pool, meaning a race condition where `currentIssueKey` reads a stale value and the guard check `if (currentIssueKey != issueKey) return@launch` fails to abort a stale load.
evidence:
private var currentIssueKey: String? = null
...
lazyScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) {
    ...
    if (currentIssueKey != issueKey) return@launch
suggestedFix: Declare the field as `@Volatile private var currentIssueKey: String? = null`. Alternatively, use `AtomicReference<String?>` consistent with `currentUserName` which is already `AtomicReference` in the same class.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 105, 126, 747, and 770. `currentIssueKey` is a plain `var` written on the EDT and read from `Dispatchers.IO` after a 200ms delay. The JVM memory model does not guarantee visibility of an EDT write to an IO-thread read without a happens-before edge. The initial write at line 126 is ordered before the `launch` at line 745 by program order within `showIssue()`, so the first read in the IO coroutine sees the correct value. The stale-read risk is specifically for a SECOND `showIssue()` call that updates `currentIssueKey` on the EDT while the IO coroutine is in its 200ms delay — the guard check `if (currentIssueKey != issueKey) return@launch` at line 747 might not observe the updated key and fail to abort the stale load. In practice the consequence is a redundant/stale UI refresh (not data loss or crash). `@Volatile` would close the gap. Severity is Low given the benign worst case (extra render of a previous ticket's data, quickly overwritten).
recommendedAction: Change line 105 to `@Volatile private var currentIssueKey: String? = null`. This is consistent with `currentUserName` being `AtomicReference` in the same class and costs nothing.

title: SprintPaginationCache.getCachedStartAt calls getOrLoadCache() OUTSIDE the synchronized(lock) block, creating a TOCTOU window
description: Lines 50-56: `getCachedStartAt` calls `getOrLoadCache()` at line 51, then enters `synchronized(lock)` at line 52 to access `data.boards[...]`. `getOrLoadCache()` itself is internally synchronized and returns a `CacheData` reference. However, between the return from `getOrLoadCache()` and entry into the inner `synchronized(lock)` block, `saveCachedStartAt()` on another thread can replace `cache` with a NEW `CacheData` object. The `data` local variable still holds the old object, so `data.boards[boardId.toString()]` reads from stale state and the startAt returned may be one update behind.
suggestedFix: Inline the read into a single synchronized block: `synchronized(lock) { val data = getOrLoadCache(); val entry = data.boards[boardId.toString()] ?: return 0; return (entry.lastStartAt - pageSize).coerceAtLeast(0) }`. `getOrLoadCache` is re-entrant-safe because it checks `cache?.let { return it }` at the top.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 50-55. `getOrLoadCache()` is called at line 51 OUTSIDE `synchronized(lock)`, returning a `CacheData` reference. The `synchronized(lock)` block is entered at line 52. In the narrow window between lines 51 and 52, `saveCachedStartAt()` (lines 62-75) can: enter `synchronized(lock)`, call `getOrLoadCache()` (returns a new `CacheData` from disk since `cache` was null), mutate its `boards` map in-place, set `cache = data`, and exit the lock. The `data` local variable in `getCachedStartAt` then refers to the pre-save object. However, since `saveCachedStartAt` mutates the boards map IN-PLACE on the same `CacheData` object and sets `cache` to that same object, and `getOrLoadCache()` in the `getCachedStartAt` call had already returned a reference to the same (or equivalent) object before the save, the practical staleness is one save behind at most. Real but very low impact: at worst the user starts a sprint-pagination walk one page too early, immediately self-correcting on the next save cycle. Severity reassessed to Low.
recommendedAction: Move the `getOrLoadCache()` call inside `synchronized(lock)` at line 52: `synchronized(lock) { val data = getOrLoadCache(); val entry = data.boards[boardId.toString()] ?: return 0; return (entry.lastStartAt - pageSize).coerceAtLeast(0) }`. `getOrLoadCache()` is already synchronized internally and is re-entrant-safe.

title: TimeTrackingCheckinHandler and PostCommitTransitionHandler register a new Disposer callback on every commit, accumulating callbacks for the project's lifetime
description: Lines 110-111: every call to `checkinSuccessful()` creates a new `CoroutineScope` and registers a new `Disposer.register(project as Disposable) { scope.cancel(...) }` lambda. The project's Disposer tree accumulates one entry per commit for the project's lifetime. After many commits, this is a slow memory leak (lambda references to dead scopes). `PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory.kt` lines 47-48 has the same pattern. Additionally, `project as Disposable` is an unsafe cast — `Project` does not implement `Disposable` in all IntelliJ platform versions and can throw `ClassCastException` on some versions.
suggestedFix: Instead of creating a scope per invocation, inject the project-level `CoroutineScope` via the service container or use `project.coroutineScope` (available from platform 2024.1+). If a per-invocation scope is needed, register it against a `Disposable` that itself is already project-scoped, not the project directly. Use `Disposer.newDisposable("time-tracking-commit")` registered under the project.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed at lines 110-111 in TimeTrackingCheckinHandlerFactory.kt and lines 47-48 in PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory.kt. Each `checkinSuccessful()` call registers a new lambda closure in the project's Disposer tree via `Disposer.register(project as Disposable)`. After N commits, there are N lambda entries in the tree even though each associated `CoroutineScope` has already completed. This is a slow memory leak (closure captures the dead scope). However: (a) each leaked entry is tiny (a few hundred bytes), (b) commits are infrequent human actions (not a tight loop), so in practice a developer would need thousands of commits in one session to see measurable pressure. The `project as Disposable` cast is safe for IntelliJ 2023+ (target is 2025.1+). Severity is Low rather than Medium given the extremely slow accumulation rate. The fix (using `project.coroutineScope` from platform 2024.1+) is correct and non-breaking.
recommendedAction: Replace the per-invocation `CoroutineScope` + `Disposer.register` pattern in both files with `project.coroutineScope.launch(Dispatchers.IO) { ... }`. This eliminates the per-commit Disposer entry since `project.coroutineScope` is already lifecycle-managed by the platform. If the platform version constraint makes `project.coroutineScope` unavailable, use a single `@Service`-level scope injected via the service container instead.

title: BranchChangeTicketDetector reads GitRepository.currentBranchName outside readAction, violating platform threading contract
description: Line 50: `val changedRepo = gitRepos.find { it.currentBranchName == branchName }` reads `GitRepository.currentBranchName` directly in `branchHasChanged`. The IntelliJ platform requires that VCS model state (GitRepository state including `currentBranchName`) be accessed only under a read lock via `readAction { }` or `runReadAction { }`. Accessing it without a lock on the event dispatch thread can produce stale values; calling it from any non-EDT context (BranchChangeListener callbacks can arrive on various threads in platform 2024+) without a read lock is a potential threading violation that can cause `ConcurrentModificationException` in GitRepositoryImpl's internal mutable state.
evidence:
val gitRepos = GitRepositoryManager.getInstance(project).repositories
val changedRepo = gitRepos.find { it.currentBranchName == branchName }
suggestedFix: Wrap the repositories access and branch comparison in a `readAction`: `val changedRepo = readAction { gitRepos.find { it.currentBranchName == branchName } }`. Note `GitRepositoryManager.getInstance(project).repositories` at line 49 should also be inside the same `readAction` block.
verdictReasoning: BranchChangeTicketDetector is registered as a project listener on the VCS_BRANCH_CHANGED topic (plugin-withGit.xml line 14-15). In IntelliJ platform 2024+, BranchChangeListener callbacks can arrive on a pooled background thread. Line 49 calls `GitRepositoryManager.getInstance(project).repositories` and line 50 reads `it.currentBranchName` without a `readAction {}` wrapper. GitRepository state is protected by the platform's read/write lock. However, in practice `currentBranchName` is a simple `@Volatile` field in GitRepositoryImpl and `GitRepositoryManager.repositories` returns an unmodifiable snapshot, making a `ConcurrentModificationException` extremely unlikely in current Git4Idea versions. The real risk is stale values on non-EDT threads, not a crash. Severity is Low rather than Medium because no user-visible misbehavior has been observed and the data accessed is append-only (single writer: platform VCS).
recommendedAction: Wrap lines 49-50 in `readAction { ... }` as suggested. Low-risk correctness fix.

title: Dead private field `availableSprints` — assigned but never read
description: The field `private var availableSprints: List<JiraSprint> = emptyList()` (line 130) is written once at line 610 (`availableSprints = sprints`) but is never read anywhere in the class or its inner classes. The sprint selector (`sprintSelector`) and the load logic both drive off `sprintService.activeSprint` and the local `sprints` local variable. The field accumulates stale list state silently.
evidence:
private var availableSprints: List<JiraSprint> = emptyList()  // line 130
...
availableSprints = sprints  // line 610 — only write, no reads
suggestedFix: Delete lines 130 and 610 (`private var availableSprints` declaration and the assignment in `populateSprintSelector`). No callers reference this field.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `private var availableSprints: List<JiraSprint> = emptyList()` is declared at line 130. The only write is at line 610 inside `populateSprintSelector`. A grep over the entire file confirms zero read sites. The sprint selector and load logic operate entirely on the local `sprints` parameter and `sprintService.activeSprint`. The field accumulates stale state silently.
recommendedAction: Delete the field declaration (line 130) and the assignment (line 610). Safe cleanup with no behavioral change.

title: Dead local variable `finalBranchClient` — always equal to `branchClient`, assigned but never used differently
description: At lines 1150-1154, `finalBranchClient` is set by a conditional whose both branches evaluate to the same value (`branchClient`). The comment even acknowledges 'create a new branch client isn't needed'. The variable is then passed to `branchingService.startWork(branchClient = finalBranchClient, ...)` at line 1188. Using `branchClient` directly is identical in effect; `finalBranchClient` adds zero semantic distinction and the comment is misleading.
suggestedFix: Delete the `finalBranchClient` variable and pass `branchClient` directly at line 1188: `branchClient = branchClient`. The comment and the if-expression can be removed entirely.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: both branches of the if-expression at lines 1150-1154 evaluate to `branchClient` (the same local variable). The comment even admits 'create a new branch client isn't needed'. The variable `finalBranchClient` introduces noise without providing any semantic distinction. It is passed to `branchingService.startWork` at line 1188 but could be replaced directly with `branchClient`.
recommendedAction: Delete the `finalBranchClient` variable and the if-expression (lines 1150-1154); pass `branchClient` directly at line 1188. Safe cleanup.

title: Duplicate `isHeader` helper — identical implementation in two sibling UI classes
description: `private fun isHeader(issue: JiraIssue): Boolean = issue.id.startsWith("header-")` appears identically at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:205 and TicketListCellRenderer.kt:55. Any change to the header-sentinel prefix (e.g., changing `"header-"` to `"section-"`) must be duplicated. The two classes are both `ui/` layer and work with the same `JiraIssue` DTO.
suggestedFix: Extract a package-level top-level function `fun JiraIssue.isSectionHeader(): Boolean = id.startsWith("header-")` in a shared file (e.g., `ui/IssueUiUtils.kt`) and replace both private usages with it. This makes the convention a single source of truth.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `private fun isHeader(issue: JiraIssue): Boolean = issue.id.startsWith("header-")` appears identically at SprintDashboardPanel.kt line 205 and TicketListCellRenderer.kt line 55. Both are private to their own class. Any future change to the 'header-' sentinel prefix must be made in two places. The finding is accurate.
recommendedAction: Extract a package-level extension function (e.g., `fun JiraIssue.isSectionHeader() = id.startsWith("header-")` in a shared `IssueUiUtils.kt`) and update both call sites. Low-risk refactor.

title: Duplicate private `CacheEntry<T>` data class in `JiraServiceImpl` and `JiraSearchServiceImpl`
description: An identical private generic `data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` is defined independently inside both `JiraServiceImpl` (line 1282) and `JiraSearchServiceImpl` (line 65). A third, non-generic variant exists in `TicketTransitionServiceImpl` (line 80). The two generic variants are character-for-character identical.
suggestedFix: Extract the generic variant to a package-level or `internal` class in the `jira.service` package (e.g., an `internal data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` in a new `CacheUtils.kt`). Both services reference this one type. `TicketTransitionServiceImpl`'s non-generic variant can be left or migrated as a follow-up.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: `private data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` is defined identically at JiraServiceImpl.kt line 1282 and JiraSearchServiceImpl.kt line 65. TicketTransitionServiceImpl.kt line 80 has a non-generic variant `private data class CacheEntry(val value: List<TransitionMeta>, val expiresAt: Long)`. The two generic variants are character-for-character identical as stated. The duplication is a maintenance hazard but causes no runtime problem today.
recommendedAction: Extract an `internal data class CacheEntry<T>(val value: T, val expiresAt: Long)` to a shared `jira/service/CacheUtils.kt` file and replace both private usages. The TicketTransitionServiceImpl non-generic variant can stay or be migrated as a follow-up.

title: Unused `project: Project` constructor parameter in `SavedFiltersSection`
description: The `project: Project` parameter at line 33 is annotated `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER")` and is not referenced anywhere inside the class. The section was designed to be callback-driven (the parent `SprintDashboardPanel` owns the `JiraService` calls and passes results via `update()`), so the `project` parameter was never needed.
suggestedFix: Remove the `project: Project` parameter from the `SavedFiltersSection` constructor and update the call site in `SprintDashboardPanel` (line 421) to `SavedFiltersSection { filter -> onFilterClicked(filter) }`. No functionality changes.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 33 of SavedFiltersSection.kt shows `@Suppress("UNUSED_PARAMETER") project: Project` is never referenced in the class body. The companion object at lines 103-119 contains the load-bearing `shouldShowSection()` static method and `MAX_DESC_CHARS` constant, but these are accessed directly as static members (SprintFavouritesRenderingTest calls `SavedFiltersSection.shouldShowSection(...)` without instantiating the class), so they are unaffected by removing the constructor parameter. The call site at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:421 currently passes `project` and would need to be updated to `SavedFiltersSection { filter -> onFilterClicked(filter) }`. No logic change, no test breakage.
recommendedAction: Remove the `project: Project` parameter from the `SavedFiltersSection` constructor and update the call site at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:421.

title: Empty `companion object` in `SprintDashboardPanel` — no-op declaration
description: Line 1236 declares `companion object` with no members. An empty `companion object` in Kotlin is unnecessary: it generates a synthetic inner class `Companion` in the bytecode for no benefit. No external code accesses `SprintDashboardPanel.Companion` or `SprintDashboardPanel` via static members.
evidence:
// SprintDashboardPanel.kt:1236
companion object
suggestedFix: Delete the empty `companion object` declaration at line 1236.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. Line 1236 of SprintDashboardPanel.kt is an empty `companion object` with zero members. No external code accesses `SprintDashboardPanel.Companion`. Deleting it removes a no-op synthetic inner class from the bytecode with zero functional impact. Note that the companion object in SavedFiltersSection (lines 103-119) is NOT empty — it holds `MAX_DESC_CHARS` and the `@JvmStatic shouldShowSection()` method used by tests, so the finding is correctly scoped to SprintDashboardPanel only.
recommendedAction: Delete the empty `companion object` declaration at SprintDashboardPanel.kt:1236.

title: `WorklogSection` bypasses `JiraService.getWorklogs` and calls raw `JiraApiClient` directly
description: `WorklogSection.loadWorklogs` calls `jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient()` (line 62) then `apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)` (line 71), consuming the raw transport DTO `JiraWorklogResponse`/`JiraWorklog`. The core `JiraService` interface already exposes `getWorklogs(issueKey): ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>` (implemented in `JiraServiceImpl` at line 450 with proper field mapping and `.summary`). The three sibling sections (`DevStatusSection`, `ChangelogSection`, `LinkedDocsSection`) all correctly use `project.getService(JiraService::class.java)`. `WorklogSection` is the sole outlier, duplicating DTO-to-display field mapping and coupling the UI to the raw transport layer.
evidence:
val jiraServiceImpl = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)  // line 61
val apiClient = jiraServiceImpl.getApiClient()  // line 62
...
val result = apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)  // line 71 — raw ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse>
suggestedFix: Replace lines 61-89 with `project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey)`, branch on `ToolResult.isError`, and render from `List<WorklogData>` (mirroring the pattern in `ChangelogSection.loadHistory`). Remove the `import com.workflow.orchestrator.jira.service.JiraServiceImpl` and the raw DTO import `JiraWorklog`.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real. WorklogSection.kt lines 61-62 retrieve `JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient()`, then line 71 calls `apiClient.getWorklogs(issueKey)` returning raw `ApiResult<JiraWorklogResponse>`. `JiraService.getWorklogs(issueKey): ToolResult<List<WorklogData>>` exists (JiraServiceImpl line 450). Sibling sections ChangelogSection uses `project.getService(JiraService::class.java)` correctly. The fix is safe but non-trivial: `renderWorklogs()` at line 102 accepts `List<JiraWorklog>` and accesses `.timeSpentSeconds`, `.author?.displayName`, `.started`, `.comment` — these fields would need to be mapped from `WorklogData`. The suggested fix undersells the effort required. Classified as Low (cleanup) rather than High because worklogs are a non-critical read-only display feature and the raw client call works correctly in practice.
recommendedAction: Replace the `getApiClient()` chain in `loadWorklogs()` with `project.getService(JiraService::class.java).getWorklogs(issueKey)` and rewrite `renderWorklogs()` to accept `List<WorklogData>` instead of `List<JiraWorklog>`.

title: SprintTabProvider wires raw JiraApiClient into SprintService and BranchingService, creating unregistered service objects
description: SprintTabProvider calls JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getApiClient() and passes the raw client into manually-constructed SprintService and BranchingService objects that are not registered as project services. These objects hold long-lived HTTP clients and coroutine scopes tied to the raw API client, meaning lifecycle is ad-hoc. The CLAUDE.md convention requires service-layer objects to be project-level IntelliJ services so the platform manages their lifecycle.
evidence:
val jiraService = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)
val apiClient = jiraService.getApiClient()
    ?: return EmptyStatePanel(...)
val sprintService = SprintService(apiClient)
val branchingService = BranchingService(project, apiClient, activeTicketService)
suggestedFix: Register SprintService and BranchingService as @Service(Service.Level.PROJECT) with lazy JiraApiClient resolution (same pattern as JiraServiceImpl.client), or inject them from plugin.xml. SprintTabProvider should retrieve them via project.getService(). This ensures lifecycle is tied to project open/close, not to tool-window panel construction.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed real: SprintTabProvider.kt lines 26-32 manually construct `SprintService(apiClient)` and `BranchingService(project, apiClient, activeTicketService)` without registering them as platform services. Neither class has `@Service`, and neither appears in plugin.xml. However, severity is significantly lower than claimed: both classes have no coroutine scopes, no `Disposable` implementation, and no long-lived state that would leak across project open/close (SprintService holds a cached `List<JiraIssue>` and a `JiraSprint?`; BranchingService is stateless). The lifecycle risk cited in the finding does not materialize because these objects are field-held by `SprintDashboardPanel`, which is itself `Disposable` and GC-eligible after tool window close. The CLAUDE.md convention requiring platform-managed lifecycle applies primarily to services holding coroutine scopes or HTTP clients as long-lived resources — SprintService wraps an existing shared `JiraApiClient` reference. Severity reassessed from High to Low.
recommendedAction: Low priority. Optionally register SprintService as a project-level @Service that lazily resolves JiraApiClient, and retrieve it via project.getService(). This is a maintenance improvement, not an urgent fix.

title: Multiple UI and listener files call JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() (concrete class) instead of project.getService(JiraService::class.java)
description: DevStatusSection, SprintDashboardPanel (loadFavouriteFilters/onFilterClicked), JiraWorkflowConfigurable (searchBoards, getFields), BranchChangeTicketDetector, PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory, and TimeTrackingCheckinHandlerFactory all call JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() to obtain the service, creating direct compile-time dependencies on the concrete implementation class from UI/listener layers. The JiraService interface exposes all needed methods (getTicket, getFields, searchBoards, getFavouriteFilters, getFilter, getFullDevStatus). Only invalidateFieldsCache and getApiClient are impl-specific; the former should be added to the interface or moved to a settings-specific seam.
evidence:
val service = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project)  // DevStatusSection.kt:60
val result = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).getTicket(ticketId)  // BranchChangeTicketDetector.kt:79
val result = JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project).searchBoards(searchText)  // JiraWorkflowConfigurable.kt:176
suggestedFix: Replace all JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project) calls outside the service package with project.getService(JiraService::class.java). For invalidateFieldsCache (settings page only), expose it on JiraService interface, or have JiraWorkflowConfigurable inject JiraServiceImpl via the same cast pattern already used in JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() companion (safe within the module). This decouples UI/listener layers from the implementation class.
verdictReasoning: The finding is real: DevStatusSection.kt line 60, BranchChangeTicketDetector.kt line 79, SprintDashboardPanel.kt lines 670/688, JiraWorkflowConfigurable.kt lines 176/452/399 all call JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project). However, the severity must be downgraded from Medium. JiraServiceImpl.getInstance() is itself implemented as `project.getService(JiraService::class.java) as JiraServiceImpl` (JiraServiceImpl.kt line 1852), so the DI container is still used — the concrete class is introduced only via an explicit cast in the companion, not by bypassing the container. This is intentional: callers that need impl-specific methods (invalidateFieldsCache at line 399 in JiraWorkflowConfigurable, which is not on the JiraService interface) genuinely need the cast. Callers that only need interface methods (DevStatusSection, BranchChangeTicketDetector, SprintDashboardPanel) could indeed use project.getService(JiraService::class.java) instead and would be cleaner, but no functional bug or brittleness results because the companion method already does the same lookup internally. The suggested fix is architecturally sound (decouple UI/listener layers from the concrete class) but carries no correctness risk, no test failures, and no user-visible impact. isHarmfulToFix=false.
recommendedAction: Low-priority cleanup: replace JiraServiceImpl.getInstance(project) calls in DevStatusSection, BranchChangeTicketDetector, SprintDashboardPanel, PostCommitTransitionHandlerFactory, and TimeTrackingCheckinHandlerFactory with project.getService(JiraService::class.java). For JiraWorkflowConfigurable's invalidateFieldsCache call (line 399), either add the method to the JiraService interface or keep the one necessary cast there. Not urgent.

title: TicketDetailPanel and QuickCommentPanel use plain javax.swing.JButton instead of JetBrains platform button
description: addTransitionAndWatchButtons() in TicketDetailPanel instantiates javax.swing.JButton directly for the Transition and Watch buttons (lines 237 and 254). QuickCommentPanel also declares sendButton = JButton(AllIcons.Actions.Execute). The project convention requires JetBrains components only. Plain JButton does not respect the IDE theme (light/dark borders, focus handling, DPI scaling) the way IntelliJ ActionButton or JBButton / platform-themed JButton wrappers do.
suggestedFix: Replace javax.swing.JButton with com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton (or use ActionButton/ActionToolbar for icon-only cases). For the Transition and Watch buttons in TicketDetailPanel, JBButton is the minimal fix. For the icon-only sendButton in QuickCommentPanel, consider ActionButton wrapping an AnAction, which also integrates with the platform's presentation/tooltip system.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: TicketDetailPanel.kt line 237 instantiates `javax.swing.JButton("${issue.fields.status.name} ▾")` and line 254 instantiates `javax.swing.JButton("👁 Watch")`. QuickCommentPanel.kt line 42 declares `private val sendButton = JButton(AllIcons.Actions.Execute)` (import is plain javax.swing.JButton at line 26). The project convention says JetBrains components only (JBButton). Plain JButton does not adapt to IDE theme scaling, font DPI, or focus handling correctly on HiDPI or non-default themes. This is a real violation of the stated UX convention. However, it is a visual/polish issue only — no crash, no data loss, no incorrect behavior. isHarmfulToFix=false: replacing with JBButton does not break any passing test or load-bearing fallback.
recommendedAction: Replace javax.swing.JButton with com.intellij.ui.components.JBButton in TicketDetailPanel (lines 237, 254) and QuickCommentPanel (line 42). For the icon-only sendButton consider ActionButton wrapping an AnAction for full platform integration.

title: JiraWorkflowConfigurable uses plain javax.swing.JTextField and JComboBox inside the Kotlin UI DSL panel
description: The settings page uses javax.swing.JTextField for boardSearchField (line 118), ticketKeyRegexField (line 149), epicManualField, and acceptanceManualField (lines 363, 388); it also uses javax.swing.JComboBox for epicCombo and acceptanceCombo (lines 355, 380). These plain Swing components do not inherit IDE theming (placeholder text, border scaling, validation decorators). The Kotlin UI DSL's textField() and comboBox() helpers should be used instead, which already respect the IntelliJ Platform look and feel.
suggestedFix: Use JBTextField for the text fields (com.intellij.ui.components.JBTextField) and ComboBox from the IntelliJ Platform (com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox) for the combo boxes, or bind them using the Kotlin UI DSL row { textField().bindText(...) } / row { comboBox(...).bindItem(...) } pattern already used elsewhere in the same file. This is especially important for the manual field fallbacks (epicManualField, acceptanceManualField) as they already reference the JiraWorkflowConfigurable's scope.
verdictReasoning: Confirmed: JiraWorkflowConfigurable.kt uses javax.swing.JTextField at lines 118 (boardSearchField), 149 (regexField), 363 (epicManualField), 388 (acceptanceManualField), and javax.swing.JComboBox at lines 121 (boardComboBox), 355 (epicCombo), 380 (acceptanceCombo). While JBTextField and com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox are the JetBrains-native replacements, using the plain Swing variants inside a Kotlin UI DSL panel is common in IntelliJ plugin code for components not bound via DSL bindText()/bindItem(). The functional impact is limited to minor visual inconsistency on non-default themes or HiDPI displays. No crash, no data loss. The board combo and epic/acceptance combos are used with SimpleListCellRenderer.create() and bindBoundedWidth() helpers that work with both types. isHarmfulToFix=false: substituting JBTextField/ComboBox does not break behavior.
recommendedAction: Low-priority cleanup: replace javax.swing.JTextField with JBTextField (com.intellij.ui.components.JBTextField) for boardSearchField, regexField, epicManualField, acceptanceManualField; replace javax.swing.JComboBox with com.intellij.openapi.ui.ComboBox for boardComboBox, epicCombo, acceptanceCombo. This is purely cosmetic but keeps the codebase consistent with the JetBrains-only UI convention.
